lesson.title: Probability
lesson.body: **Probability** measures how likely an event is: P(event) = favorable outcomes ÷ total outcomes. It's always between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%). P = 0 means impossible; P = 1 means certain. The **complement** of event A is everything that is NOT A: P(not A) = 1 − P(A). **Experimental probability** is based on observed data from trials. **Theoretical probability** assumes all outcomes are equally likely. The more trials you run, the closer experimental approaches theoretical.

rooms.question: A bag has 3 red, 5 blue, and 2 green marbles. What is the probability of picking a blue marble?
rooms.explanation: P(blue) = 5/10 = 1/2. Total outcomes = 10 (all marbles). Favorable = 5 (blue). The 1/3 error treats each color as equally likely (3 colors → 1/3 each), ignoring the different counts.

rooms.question: The probability of rain tomorrow is 0.35. What is the probability it does NOT rain?
rooms.explanation: Complement rule: P(not A) = 1 − P(A) = 1 − 0.35 = 0.65. Rain and no rain are complementary events — exactly one must occur. Their probabilities must always sum to 1.

rooms.question: A die is rolled 60 times. It lands on 3 exactly 8 times. What is the experimental probability of rolling a 3?
rooms.explanation: Experimental probability = 8/60 = 2/15 ≈ 0.133. The theoretical probability is 1/6 ≈ 0.167. They differ because 60 trials is not enough to guarantee the exact theoretical ratio. With millions of trials, experimental probability converges to 1/6.

rooms.question: A spinner has 8 equal sections: 3 red, 2 blue, 2 yellow, 1 green. What is the probability of landing on red OR yellow?
rooms.explanation: Red has 3 sections, yellow has 2. They don't overlap, so P(red or yellow) = (3+2)/8 = 5/8. This is the addition rule for mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) when A and B can't happen simultaneously.

rooms.question: Order these events from least likely (lowest probability) to most likely:
items: Rolling a 6 on a die: P = 1/6 ≈ 0.17
Flipping tails on a fair coin: P = 1/2 = 0.5
Drawing a face card from a standard deck: P = 12/52 ≈ 0.23
Drawing any card from a full deck: P = 1
rooms.explanation: Order by probability: 1/6 ≈ 0.17 < 12/52 ≈ 0.23 < 1/2 = 0.5 < 1. Note that 12/52 = 3/13 ≈ 0.23 (there are 12 face cards in a 52-card deck). The certain event (drawing any card) always has P = 1.

boss.name: The Probability Shade
boss.taunt: The odds are not in your favor.

questions.question: A class of 30 students: 12 play sports, 10 play instruments, 4 do both. What is the probability a randomly chosen student plays sports OR instruments?
questions.latex: P(S \cup I) = P(S) + P(I) - P(S \cap I)
questions.explanation: P(sports or instruments) = P(S) + P(I) − P(both) = 12/30 + 10/30 − 4/30 = 18/30 = 3/5. Subtracting P(both) prevents double-counting the 4 students who do both. The 22/30 error adds without subtracting the overlap.

questions.question: A box has 4 black and 6 white socks. You draw one without looking. What is the probability it is black, given that it is NOT white? (Interpret this as: you already know it's not white.)
questions.explanation: If you already know the sock is not white, then only the 4 black socks remain as possibilities. P(black | not white) = 4/4 = 1 (certain). When you condition on 'not white,' you eliminate all 6 white socks from the sample space.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 4 times. Theoretically, how many times would you expect heads in 400 total flips?
questions.explanation: Expected value = total trials × probability = 400 × 1/2 = 200. This is the long-run average, not a guaranteed outcome. In any 400 actual flips, you might get 195 or 207 — but the average over many sets of 400 flips approaches 200.
